living-concrete.knowledge:
Craik & Lockhart (1972): information processed at deeper levels (semantic, relational) is retained longer than information processed shallowly (phonological, surface features). This is not preference -- it is how encoding works. Deeper processing = more durable memory trace.
Three levels: - SURFACE: Physical features (font, layout, sequence of presentation) - INTERMEDIATE: Semantic meaning (what does this term mean?) - DEEP: Relational structure (how does this connect to other knowledge?)
Most information processing defaults to intermediate. The skill is deliberately operating at the deep level.

Bloom's Taxonomy (revised 2001): Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create. Traditional teaching: master lower before attempting higher.
Sung's inversion (via HOL research): Enter at Analyze/Evaluate/Create. The brain automatically fills in Remember and Understand when engaged in higher-order operations. You do not need to memorize before you can analyze. Analysis PRODUCES memorization as byproduct.
This inverts the pedagogical assumption. It is more efficient to start high and let lower-order follow than to build up from rote.

Kim Peak (the real Rain Man): perfect recall of entire books, maps, atlases -- but could not reason about what he remembered. Extraordinary memory without reasoning ability limits problem-solving.
The paradox: "remembering everything" is not "remembering everything you need." Strategic retention (understanding) beats exhaustive storage.
BentOS analog: the task-tracker-mem antipattern. A .mem that records every event but encodes no understanding is Kim Peak's mind -- perfect recall, zero reasoning.
living-concrete.pattern:
When processing any information, ask relational questions: - What CAUSES this? What does this CAUSE? - What CONTAINS this? What does this CONTAIN? - What CONTRADICTS this? - What is this ANALOGOUS to? Where does the analogy BREAK? - What DEPENDS on this? What does this DEPEND on?
These questions force deep encoding. The answers become the structure that makes knowledge retrievable and usable.

When consolidating knowledge (writing .mem, summarizing, documenting): REWRITE from understanding, don't APPEND chronologically.
Chronological append = surface encoding (preserves sequence of events). Organic rewrite = deep encoding (preserves structure of understanding).
This is the formal basis for BentOS organic-rewrite. It is not a stylistic preference -- it is encoding science.

Self-monitoring question during any cognitive work: "Am I processing structure or transcribing surface?"
Signals of surface processing: - Output mirrors input sequence (same order as consumed) - Facts listed without relationships - No contradictions or tensions surfaced - Could have been produced without understanding
Signals of deep processing: - Output restructured by relationship, not consumption order - Connections, causes, dependencies made explicit - Tensions and contradictions named - Could NOT have been produced without understanding
living-concrete.antipattern: DON'T: Build up from rote memorization hoping analysis will follow. DO: Start with analysis -- recall emerges as byproduct. Bloom's inversion: higher-order first, not last.
